{{Shinto Shrine | name = Seibo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | Alternate names = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Shrine | name = Seibo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | Alternate names = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} revision.comment: Text replacement - "== 文化財 ==" to "== Cultural Properties =="
revision.text:
{{神社 |name = 聖母宮 |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|拝殿]]
拝殿 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|location = 長崎県壱岐市勝本町勝本浦554-2 |位置 = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=聖母宮}} |deity = 息長足姫尊
足仲彦尊
住吉大神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|type = 式内大社（名神大）・壱岐国二宮・旧郷社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = [[流造|三間社流造]] |別名 = |札所等 = 壱岐国大七社 |festival = 10月14日 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki Prefecture|label=聖母宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|鳥居]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|西門]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|本殿
本拝側面は覆いにより雨風から保護されており、屋根のみ露出している。]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|馬蹄石
神功皇后が御乗馬した際の足跡が残るとされている。]] '''聖母宮'''（しょうもぐう）は、[[長崎県]][[壱岐市]]勝本町勝本浦に鎮座する[[神社]]。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』に載せる[[壱岐島]][[壱岐郡]]の[[名神大社]]、「'''中津神社'''」の有力[[論社]]であるが、現在その社名の別神社（[[中津神社 (壱岐市)|中津神社]]）が存在している。 == deity == '''息長足姫尊'''（おきながたらしひめのみこと。[[神功皇后]]）と'''足仲彦尊'''（たらしなかつひこのみこと。[[仲哀天皇]]）と、'''上筒男尊'''（うわつつのおのみこと）、'''仲筒男尊'''（なかつつのおのみこと）、'''底筒男尊'''（そこつつのおのみこと）の[[住吉三神]]（住吉大神）をdeityとする。 次に見るように神功皇后の[[三韓征伐]]に因む神社で、征伐に勝利したことから勝負の神として知られる。また、皇后は三韓征伐の際に[[応神天皇]]を身籠り、凱旋の後に無事に出産したとの伝えから安産の神としても知られる。 == History == [[仲哀天皇]]9年（[[200年]]？）10月、[[神功皇后]]が[[三韓征伐]]の折、壱岐で風待ちをした時に[[行宮]]を建てたのが起源とされる。この時神功皇后は北へ向かうのに良い風が吹いたことから、この地を風本（かざもと）と名付けた。同年12月三韓から凱旋時に勝利を記念して、風本を勝本に改めた。その時に敵の首101500をこの地に埋めたという伝承が残る。その後行宮は放置されたが、海より光るものが上がってくる事件が起き、その際に鏡を奉納し神功皇后を祀ったとされる。 [[養老]]元年（[[717年]]）、国家鎮護が祈願されて社殿が建てられる。聖母宮ではこれをもって神社の創建としている。古来より国主の崇敬厚く壱岐七社の一つとして、また壱岐国二の宮とされた。古来より福岡市の[[香椎宮]]との繋がりが深く、香椎宮（香椎大明神）を名乗っていた時期もあり、さらに地元が香椎村を名乗っていた時期もあった。しかし当社が香椎宮の分社というわけではない。 『[[本朝神社考]]』では式内小社、手長比売神社に比定された。[[延宝]]4年の[[橘三喜]]の査定では、当社と勝本町北触の中津宮（現中津神社）を式内大社、中津神社に比定したが（手長比売神社は勝本町本宮西触の[[手長比賣神社]]に比定）、中津宮が中津神社に改称され、こちらが式内中津神社とみなされるようになった。社伝では、「過ちありて式内社の資格を失った」としている。しかし、現在の中津神社を式内中津神社とすることには否定的な意見が多く、『式内社調査報告』では聖母宮が式内中津神社であり、現在の中津神社はその分祀であろうと記している。 なお、所在地が長崎県で、神社名に「聖母」とあることから[[キリスト教]]と関係があると誤解を受けることがあるが無関係である。 == Rites == 当社のfestivalは5日間にわたって行われ、市内最大の祭りとして知られる。中でも来年が豊作か大漁かを占う[[選択無形民俗文化財]]の御神幸船は有名である。最終日の10月14日には、[[重要無形民俗文化財]][[壱岐神楽]]の大神楽や氏子の仮装パレードも行われる。一般的には10月15日に行われる勝本港祭りも一体の祭として認識されており、6日間とされる場合もある。 == 境内 == 当社の神域内に、三韓征伐の際の神功皇后の馬の蹄の跡が残っているとされる馬蹄石がある。ここは[[元寇]]の文永の役の元軍上陸地でもある。 [[豊臣秀吉]]の朝鮮出兵の際に[[加藤清正]]から正門の奉納したものであり、神社周囲の石垣も当時風待ち時に配下に積ませたものであり、朝鮮半島と風に縁のある神社である。 == Cultural Properties == （括弧内は指定の区分と年月日） === 選択無形民俗文化財（国選択） === *壱岐の船競漕行事（平成3年（1991）2月2日選択） === 長崎県指定有形文化財 === *本殿・西門・南門（建造物、平成16年（2004年）2月25日） *茶壺（工芸品）、昭和47年（1972年）2月4日） :[[唐津焼]]の黒釉四耳壺（しじこ）。天正20年（1592年）の銘があり、唐津焼の最古の在銘資料である。 == 交通 == *[[壱岐交通]]バス郷ノ浦勝本線仲折バス停 == 周辺 == *勝本港 *[[壱岐市立勝本小学校]] *[[勝本城]]跡 == 関連図書 == * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、172頁 == 外部リンク == *{{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=聖母宮 |date=20010417092136}}（神社公式） *[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 聖母宮本殿・西門・南門]、[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 聖母宮の茶壺]（長崎県「長崎県の文化財」） {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{神社 |name = 聖母宮 |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|拝殿]]
拝殿 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|location = 長崎県壱岐市勝本町勝本浦554-2 |位置 = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=聖母宮}} |deity = 息長足姫尊
足仲彦尊
住吉大神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|type = 式内大社（名神大）・壱岐国二宮・旧郷社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = [[流造|三間社流造]] |別名 = |札所等 = 壱岐国大七社 |festival = 10月14日 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki Prefecture|label=聖母宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|鳥居]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|西門]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|本殿
本拝側面は覆いにより雨風から保護されており、屋根のみ露出している。]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|馬蹄石
神功皇后が御乗馬した際の足跡が残るとされている。]] '''聖母宮'''（しょうもぐう）は、[[長崎県]][[壱岐市]]勝本町勝本浦に鎮座する[[神社]]。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』に載せる[[壱岐島]][[壱岐郡]]の[[名神大社]]、「'''中津神社'''」の有力[[論社]]であるが、現在その社名の別神社（[[中津神社 (壱岐市)|中津神社]]）が存在している。 == deity == '''息長足姫尊'''（おきながたらしひめのみこと。[[神功皇后]]）と'''足仲彦尊'''（たらしなかつひこのみこと。[[仲哀天皇]]）と、'''上筒男尊'''（うわつつのおのみこと）、'''仲筒男尊'''（なかつつのおのみこと）、'''底筒男尊'''（そこつつのおのみこと）の[[住吉三神]]（住吉大神）をdeityとする。 次に見るように神功皇后の[[三韓征伐]]に因む神社で、征伐に勝利したことから勝負の神として知られる。また、皇后は三韓征伐の際に[[応神天皇]]を身籠り、凱旋の後に無事に出産したとの伝えから安産の神としても知られる。 == History == [[仲哀天皇]]9年（[[200年]]？）10月、[[神功皇后]]が[[三韓征伐]]の折、壱岐で風待ちをした時に[[行宮]]を建てたのが起源とされる。この時神功皇后は北へ向かうのに良い風が吹いたことから、この地を風本（かざもと）と名付けた。同年12月三韓から凱旋時に勝利を記念して、風本を勝本に改めた。その時に敵の首101500をこの地に埋めたという伝承が残る。その後行宮は放置されたが、海より光るものが上がってくる事件が起き、その際に鏡を奉納し神功皇后を祀ったとされる。 [[養老]]元年（[[717年]]）、国家鎮護が祈願されて社殿が建てられる。聖母宮ではこれをもって神社の創建としている。古来より国主の崇敬厚く壱岐七社の一つとして、また壱岐国二の宮とされた。古来より福岡市の[[香椎宮]]との繋がりが深く、香椎宮（香椎大明神）を名乗っていた時期もあり、さらに地元が香椎村を名乗っていた時期もあった。しかし当社が香椎宮の分社というわけではない。 『[[本朝神社考]]』では式内小社、手長比売神社に比定された。[[延宝]]4年の[[橘三喜]]の査定では、当社と勝本町北触の中津宮（現中津神社）を式内大社、中津神社に比定したが（手長比売神社は勝本町本宮西触の[[手長比賣神社]]に比定）、中津宮が中津神社に改称され、こちらが式内中津神社とみなされるようになった。社伝では、「過ちありて式内社の資格を失った」としている。しかし、現在の中津神社を式内中津神社とすることには否定的な意見が多く、『式内社調査報告』では聖母宮が式内中津神社であり、現在の中津神社はその分祀であろうと記している。 なお、所在地が長崎県で、神社名に「聖母」とあることから[[キリスト教]]と関係があると誤解を受けることがあるが無関係である。 == Rites == 当社のfestivalは5日間にわたって行われ、市内最大の祭りとして知られる。中でも来年が豊作か大漁かを占う[[選択無形民俗文化財]]の御神幸船は有名である。最終日の10月14日には、[[重要無形民俗文化財]][[壱岐神楽]]の大神楽や氏子の仮装パレードも行われる。一般的には10月15日に行われる勝本港祭りも一体の祭として認識されており、6日間とされる場合もある。 == 境内 == 当社の神域内に、三韓征伐の際の神功皇后の馬の蹄の跡が残っているとされる馬蹄石がある。ここは[[元寇]]の文永の役の元軍上陸地でもある。 [[豊臣秀吉]]の朝鮮出兵の際に[[加藤清正]]から正門の奉納したものであり、神社周囲の石垣も当時風待ち時に配下に積ませたものであり、朝鮮半島と風に縁のある神社である。 == Cultural Properties == （括弧内は指定の区分と年月日） === 選択無形民俗文化財（国選択） === *壱岐の船競漕行事（平成3年（1991）2月2日選択） === 長崎県指定有形文化財 === *本殿・西門・南門（建造物、平成16年（2004年）2月25日） *茶壺（工芸品）、昭和47年（1972年）2月4日） :[[唐津焼]]の黒釉四耳壺（しじこ）。天正20年（1592年）の銘があり、唐津焼の最古の在銘資料である。 == 交通 == *[[壱岐交通]]バス郷ノ浦勝本線仲折バス停 == 周辺 == *勝本港 *[[壱岐市立勝本小学校]] *[[勝本城]]跡 == 関連図書 == * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、172頁 == External Links == *{{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=聖母宮 |date=20010417092136}}（神社公式） *[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 聖母宮本殿・西門・南門]、[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 聖母宮の茶壺]（長崎県「長崎県の文化財」） {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|しようもくう}} [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:壱岐市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:壱岐島の式内社|名しようもくう]] [[Category:八幡宮]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:壱岐国|社2]] [[Category:壱岐市の建築物]] [[Category:神功皇后]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:mass imported shinto cult pages needing dummy edit for attribution and to be reviewed if a redirect and jawiki link reviewing]][[ja:聖母宮]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:getting the categories and imported pages jawiki linked]][[ja:聖母宮]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages of uncertain originality]]

{{Shinto Shrine | name = Seibo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | Alternate names = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Shrine | name = Seibo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | Alternate names = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Shrine | name = Seibo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | Alternate names = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Shrine | name = Seibo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | Alternate names = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{神社 |name = 聖母宮 |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|拝殿]]
拝殿 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|location = 長崎県壱岐市勝本町勝本浦554-2 |位置 = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=聖母宮}} |deity = 息長足姫尊
足仲彦尊
住吉大神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|type = 式内大社（名神大）・壱岐国二宮・旧郷社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = [[流造|三間社流造]] |別名 = |札所等 = 壱岐国大七社 |festival = 10月14日 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki Prefecture|label=聖母宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|鳥居]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|西門]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|本殿
本拝側面は覆いにより雨風から保護されており、屋根のみ露出している。]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|馬蹄石
神功皇后が御乗馬した際の足跡が残るとされている。]] '''聖母宮'''（しょうもぐう）は、[[長崎県]][[壱岐市]]勝本町勝本浦に鎮座する[[神社]]。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』に載せる[[壱岐島]][[壱岐郡]]の[[名神大社]]、「'''中津神社'''」の有力[[論社]]であるが、現在その社名の別神社（[[中津神社 (壱岐市)|中津神社]]）が存在している。 == deity == '''息長足姫尊'''（おきながたらしひめのみこと。[[神功皇后]]）と'''足仲彦尊'''（たらしなかつひこのみこと。[[仲哀天皇]]）と、'''上筒男尊'''（うわつつのおのみこと）、'''仲筒男尊'''（なかつつのおのみこと）、'''底筒男尊'''（そこつつのおのみこと）の[[住吉三神]]（住吉大神）をdeityとする。 次に見るように神功皇后の[[三韓征伐]]に因む神社で、征伐に勝利したことから勝負の神として知られる。また、皇后は三韓征伐の際に[[応神天皇]]を身籠り、凱旋の後に無事に出産したとの伝えから安産の神としても知られる。 == History == [[仲哀天皇]]9年（[[200年]]？）10月、[[神功皇后]]が[[三韓征伐]]の折、壱岐で風待ちをした時に[[行宮]]を建てたのが起源とされる。この時神功皇后は北へ向かうのに良い風が吹いたことから、この地を風本（かざもと）と名付けた。同年12月三韓から凱旋時に勝利を記念して、風本を勝本に改めた。その時に敵の首101500をこの地に埋めたという伝承が残る。その後行宮は放置されたが、海より光るものが上がってくる事件が起き、その際に鏡を奉納し神功皇后を祀ったとされる。 [[養老]]元年（[[717年]]）、国家鎮護が祈願されて社殿が建てられる。聖母宮ではこれをもって神社の創建としている。古来より国主の崇敬厚く壱岐七社の一つとして、また壱岐国二の宮とされた。古来より福岡市の[[香椎宮]]との繋がりが深く、香椎宮（香椎大明神）を名乗っていた時期もあり、さらに地元が香椎村を名乗っていた時期もあった。しかし当社が香椎宮の分社というわけではない。 『[[本朝神社考]]』では式内小社、手長比売神社に比定された。[[延宝]]4年の[[橘三喜]]の査定では、当社と勝本町北触の中津宮（現中津神社）を式内大社、中津神社に比定したが（手長比売神社は勝本町本宮西触の[[手長比賣神社]]に比定）、中津宮が中津神社に改称され、こちらが式内中津神社とみなされるようになった。社伝では、「過ちありて式内社の資格を失った」としている。しかし、現在の中津神社を式内中津神社とすることには否定的な意見が多く、『式内社調査報告』では聖母宮が式内中津神社であり、現在の中津神社はその分祀であろうと記している。 なお、所在地が長崎県で、神社名に「聖母」とあることから[[キリスト教]]と関係があると誤解を受けることがあるが無関係である。 == Rites == 当社のfestivalは5日間にわたって行われ、市内最大の祭りとして知られる。中でも来年が豊作か大漁かを占う[[選択無形民俗文化財]]の御神幸船は有名である。最終日の10月14日には、[[重要無形民俗文化財]][[壱岐神楽]]の大神楽や氏子の仮装パレードも行われる。一般的には10月15日に行われる勝本港祭りも一体の祭として認識されており、6日間とされる場合もある。 == 境内 == 当社の神域内に、三韓征伐の際の神功皇后の馬の蹄の跡が残っているとされる馬蹄石がある。ここは[[元寇]]の文永の役の元軍上陸地でもある。 [[豊臣秀吉]]の朝鮮出兵の際に[[加藤清正]]から正門の奉納したものであり、神社周囲の石垣も当時風待ち時に配下に積ませたものであり、朝鮮半島と風に縁のある神社である。 == Cultural Properties == （括弧内は指定の区分と年月日） === 選択無形民俗文化財（国選択） === *壱岐の船競漕行事（平成3年（1991）2月2日選択） === 長崎県指定有形文化財 === *本殿・西門・南門（建造物、平成16年（2004年）2月25日） *茶壺（工芸品）、昭和47年（1972年）2月4日） :[[唐津焼]]の黒釉四耳壺（しじこ）。天正20年（1592年）の銘があり、唐津焼の最古の在銘資料である。 == 交通 == *[[壱岐交通]]バス郷ノ浦勝本線仲折バス停 == 周辺 == *勝本港 *[[壱岐市立勝本小学校]] *[[勝本城]]跡 == 関連図書 == * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、172頁 == External Links == *{{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=聖母宮 |date=20010417092136}}（神社公式） *[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 聖母宮本殿・西門・南門]、[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 聖母宮の茶壺]（長崎県「長崎県の文化財」） {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} [[仲哀天皇]]9年（[[200年]]？）10月、[[神功皇后]]が[[三韓征伐]]の折、壱岐で風待ちをした時に[[行宮]]を建てたのが起源とされる。この時神功皇后は北へ向かうのに良い風が吹いたことから、この地を風本（かざもと）と名付けた。同年12月三韓から凱旋時に勝利を記念して、風本を勝本に改めた。その時に敵の首101500をこの地に埋めたという伝承が残る。その後行宮は放置されたが、海より光るものが上がってくる事件が起き、その際に鏡を奉納し神功皇后を祀ったとされる。 [[養老]]元年（[[717年]]）、国家鎮護が祈願されて社殿が建てられる。聖母宮ではこれをもって神社の創建としている。古来より国主の崇敬厚く壱岐七社の一つとして、また壱岐国二の宮とされた。古来より福岡市の[[香椎宮]]との繋がりが深く、香椎宮（香椎大明神）を名乗っていた時期もあり、さらに地元が香椎村を名乗っていた時期もあった。しかし当社が香椎宮の分社というわけではない。 『[[本朝神社考]]』では式内小社、手長比売神社に比定された。[[延宝]]4年の[[橘三喜]]の査定では、当社と勝本町北触の中津宮（現中津神社）を式内大社、中津神社に比定したが（手長比売神社は勝本町本宮西触の[[手長比賣神社]]に比定）、中津宮が中津神社に改称され、こちらが式内中津神社とみなされるようになった。社伝では、「過ちありて式内社の資格を失った」としている。しかし、現在の中津神社を式内中津神社とすることには否定的な意見が多く、『式内社調査報告』では聖母宮が式内中津神社であり、現在の中津神社はその分祀であろうと記している。 なお、所在地が長崎県で、神社名に「聖母」とあることから[[キリスト教]]と関係があると誤解を受けることがあるが無関係である。 == Rites == 当社のfestivalは5日間にわたって行われ、市内最大の祭りとして知られる。中でも来年が豊作か大漁かを占う[[選択無形民俗文化財]]の御神幸船は有名である。最終日の10月14日には、[[重要無形民俗文化財]][[壱岐神楽]]の大神楽や氏子の仮装パレードも行われる。一般的には10月15日に行われる勝本港祭りも一体の祭として認識されており、6日間とされる場合もある。 == 境内 == 当社の神域内に、三韓征伐の際の神功皇后の馬の蹄の跡が残っているとされる馬蹄石がある。ここは[[元寇]]の文永の役の元軍上陸地でもある。 [[豊臣秀吉]]の朝鮮出兵の際に[[加藤清正]]から正門の奉納したものであり、神社周囲の石垣も当時風待ち時に配下に積ませたものであり、朝鮮半島と風に縁のある神社である。 == Cultural Properties == （括弧内は指定の区分と年月日） === 選択無形民俗文化財（国選択） === *壱岐の船競漕行事（平成3年（1991）2月2日選択） === 長崎県指定有形文化財 === *本殿・西門・南門（建造物、平成16年（2004年）2月25日） *茶壺（工芸品）、昭和47年（1972年）2月4日） :[[唐津焼]]の黒釉四耳壺（しじこ）。天正20年（1592年）の銘があり、唐津焼の最古の在銘資料である。 == 交通 == *[[壱岐交通]]バス郷ノ浦勝本線仲折バス停 == 周辺 == *勝本港 *[[壱岐市立勝本小学校]] *[[勝本城]]跡 == 関連図書 == * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、172頁 == External Links == *{{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=聖母宮 |date=20010417092136}}（神社公式） *[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 聖母宮本殿・西門・南門]、[http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 聖母宮の茶壺]（長崎県「長崎県の文化財」） {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|しようもくう}} [[Category:長崎県の神社]] [[Category:壱岐市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:壱岐島の式内社|名しようもくう]] [[Category:八幡宮]] [[Category:長崎県の旧郷社]] [[Category:壱岐国|社2]] [[Category:壱岐市の建築物]] [[Category:神功皇后]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:mass imported shinto cult pages needing dummy edit for attribution and to be reviewed if a redirect and jawiki link reviewing]][[ja:聖母宮]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:getting the categories and imported pages jawiki linked]][[ja:聖母宮]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:聖母宮]][[Category:qq]]

{{神社 |name = 聖母宮 |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|拝殿]]
拝殿 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|location = 長崎県壱岐市勝本町勝本浦554-2 |位置 = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=聖母宮}} |deity = 息長足姫尊
足仲彦尊
住吉大神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|type = 式内大社（名神大）・壱岐国二宮・旧郷社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = [[流造|三間社流造]] |別名 = |札所等 = 壱岐国大七社 |festival = 10月14日 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Nagasaki Prefecture|label=聖母宮|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|鳥居]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|西門]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|本殿
本拝側面は覆いにより雨風から保護されており、屋根のみ露出している。]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|馬蹄石
神功皇后が御乗馬した際の足跡が残るとされている。]] '''聖母宮'''（しょうもぐう）は、[[長崎県]][[壱岐市]]勝本町勝本浦に鎮座する[[神社]]。『[[延喜式神名帳]]』に載せる[[壱岐島]][[壱岐郡]]の[[名神大社]]、「'''中津神社'''」の有力[[論社]]であるが、現在その社名の別神社（[[中津神社 (壱岐市)|中津神社]]）が存在している。 == deity == '''息長足姫尊'''（おきながたらしひめのみこと。[[神功皇后]]）と'''足仲彦尊'''（たらしなかつひこのみこと。[[仲哀天皇]]）と、'''上筒男尊'''（うわつつのおのみこと）、'''仲筒男尊'''（なかつつのおのみこと）、'''底筒男尊'''（そこつつのおのみこと）の[[住吉三神]]（住吉大神）をdeityとする。 次に見るように神功皇后の[[三韓征伐]]に因む神社で、征伐に勝利したことから勝負の神として知られる。また、皇后は三韓征伐の際に[[応神天皇]]を身籠り、凱旋の後に無事に出産したとの伝えから安産の神としても知られる。 == History == [[仲哀天皇]]9年（[[200年]]？）10月、[[神功皇后]]が[[三韓征伐]]の折、壱岐で風待ちをした時に[[行宮]]を建てたのが起源とされる。この時神功皇后は北へ向かうのに良い風が吹いたことから、この地を風本（かざもと）と名付けた。同年12月三韓から凱旋時に勝利を記念して、風本を勝本に改めた。その時に敵の首101500をこの地に埋めたという伝承が残る。その後行宮は放置されたが、海より光るものが上がってくる事件が起き、その際に鏡を奉納し神功皇后を祀ったとされる。 『[[本朝神社考]]』では式内小社、手長比売神社に比定された。[[延宝]]4年の[[橘三喜]]の査定では、当社と勝本町北触の中津宮（現中津神社）を式内大社、中津神社に比定したが（手長比売神社は勝本町本宮西触の[[手長比賣神社]]に比定）、中津宮が中津神社に改称され、こちらが式内中津神社とみなされるようになった。社伝では、「過ちありて式内社の資格を失った」としている。しかし、現在の中津神社を式内中津神社とすることには否定的な意見が多く、『式内社調査報告』では聖母宮が式内中津神社であり、現在の中津神社はその分祀であろうと記している。 なお、所在地が長崎県で、神社名に「聖母」とあることから[[キリスト教]]と関係があると誤解を受けることがあるが無関係である。 == Rites == 当社のfestivalは5日間にわたって行われ、市内最大の祭りとして知られる。中でも来年が豊作か大漁かを占う[[選択無形民俗文化財]]の御神幸船は有名である。最終日の10月14日には、[[重要無形民俗文化財]][[壱岐神楽]]の大神楽や氏子の仮装パレードも行われる。一般的には10月15日に行われる勝本港祭りも一体の祭として認識されており、6日間とされる場合もある。 == 境内 == 当社の神域内に、三韓征伐の際の神功皇后の馬の蹄の跡が残っているとされる馬蹄石がある。ここは[[元寇]]の文永の役の元軍上陸地でもある。 [[豊臣秀吉]]の朝鮮出兵の際に[[加藤清正]]から正門の奉納したものであり、神社周囲の石垣も当時風待ち時に配下に積ませたものであり、朝鮮半島と風に縁のある神社である。 == Cultural Properties == （括弧内は指定の区分と年月日） === 選択無形民俗文化財（国選択） === *壱岐の船競漕行事（平成3年（1991）2月2日選択） === 長崎県指定有形文化財 === *本殿・西門・南門（建造物、平成16年（2004年）2月25日） *茶壺（工芸品）、昭和47年（1972年）2月4日） :[[唐津焼]]の黒釉四耳壺（しじこ）。天正20年（1592年）の銘があり、唐津焼の最古の在銘資料である。 == 交通 == *[[壱岐交通]]バス郷ノ浦勝本線仲折バス停 == 周辺 == *勝本港 *[[壱岐市立勝本小学校]] *[[勝本城]]跡 == 関連図書 == * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、172頁 == External Links == *{{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=聖母宮 {{Shinto Shrine | name = Seibo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | Alternate names = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | Alternate names = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | Alternate names = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Shrine | name = Seibo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=draft}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|12=draft}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of
