The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|12=draft}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=draft}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=draft}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=draft}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=draft}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=draft}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=draft}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=draft}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|12=draft}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告|12=draft}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=draft}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|12=draft}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|12=draft}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=draft}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{Shinto Shrine | name = Seibo Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG|250px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|12=draft}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=draft}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=draft}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=draft}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国|12=draft}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|12=draft}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=draft}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=draft}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=draft}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=draft}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=draft}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=draft}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=draft}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|12=draft}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告|12=draft}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=draft}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|12=draft}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|12=draft}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=draft}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=draft}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城|12=draft}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Disambiguation}} {{citation needed|Nakatsu Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Seibo Shrines]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrines]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:Completed disambiguations]] [[Category:11]] {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=draft}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国|12=draft}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|12=draft}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=draft}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=draft}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=draft}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=draft}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=draft}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=draft}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=draft}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|12=draft}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告|12=draft}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=draft}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|12=draft}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|12=draft}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=draft}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=draft}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城|12=draft}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Disambiguation}} {{citation needed|Nakatsu Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Seibo Shrines]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrines]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:Completed disambiguations]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:聖母宮]] [[en:Seibo Shrines]] [[ja:中津神社]] [[sn:Seibo Shrine]] [[sn:eibo Shrine]]

In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|12=draft}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告|12=draft}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=draft}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|12=draft}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|12=draft}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=draft}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=draft}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城|12=draft}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Disambiguation}} {{citation needed|Nakatsu Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Seibo {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=draft}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国|12=draft}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|12=draft}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=draft}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=draft}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=draft}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=draft}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=draft}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=draft}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=draft}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|12=draft}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告|12=draft}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=draft}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|12=draft}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|12=draft}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=draft}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=draft}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城|12=draft}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|12=draft|12=draft}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=draft|12=draft}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=draft|12=draft}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=draft|12=draft}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国|12=draft|12=draft}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft|12=draft}} (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=draft|12=draft}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=draft|12=draft}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=draft|12=draft}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=draft|12=draft}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=draft|12=draft}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=draft|12=draft}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=draft|12=draft}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|12=draft|12=draft}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告|12=draft|12=draft}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|12=draft|12=draft}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft|12=draft}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|12=draft|12=draft}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=draft|12=draft}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城|12=draft|12=draft}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Disambiguation}} {{citation needed|Nakatsu Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Seibo Shrines]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrines]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:Completed disambiguations]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Seibo Shrines]] [[Category:Seibo Shrine forks]] [[Category:Seibo Shrine]] [[Category:All disambiguation pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:聖母宮]] [[en:Seibo Shrines]] [[ja:中津神社]] [[sn:Seibo Shrine]] [[sn:eibo Shrine]]

The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|12=draft|12=draft}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=draft|12=draft}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=draft|12=draft}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=draft|12=draft}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=draft|12=draft}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=draft|12=draft}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=draft|12=draft}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=draft|12=draft}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|12=draft|12=draft}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告|12=draft|12=draft}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|12=draft|12=draft}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|12=draft|12=draft}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|12=draft|12=draft}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=draft|12=draft}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城|12=draft|12=draft}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Disambiguation}} {{citation needed|Nakatsu Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Seibo Shrines]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrines]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:Completed disambiguations]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Seibo Shrines]] [[Category:Seibo Shrine forks]] [[Category:Seibo Shrine]] [[Category:All disambiguation pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:聖母宮]] [[en:Seibo Shrines]] [[ja:中津神社]] [[sn:Seibo Shrine]] [[sn:eibo Shrine]]

Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社調査報告|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|qq=draft|qq=draft}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Disambiguation}} {{citation needed|Nakatsu Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Seibo Shrines]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrines]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:Completed Shrines]] [[ja:中津神社]] [[sn:Seibo Shrine]] [[sn:eibo Shrine]] {{translated page|ja|聖母宮|version=89510503|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|qq=draft|qq=draft}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Disambiguation}} {{translated page|ja|聖母宮|version=89510503|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:Completed disambiguations]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Seibo Shrines]] [[Category:Seibo Shrine forks]] [[Category:Seibo Shrine]] [[Category:All disambiguation pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Seibo Shrines]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrines]] [[ja:聖母宮]] [[en:Seibo Shrines]] [[ja:中津神社]] [[sn:Seibo Shrine]] [[sn:eibo Shrine]]

Haiden | location = {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City|qq=}}, Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|ja|長崎県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaki Prefecture|qq=}} | coordinates = {{Coord|33|51|18.4|N|129|41|27.2|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Seibo Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|qq=}}

{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of {{ill|Iki Province|ja|壱岐国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Province|qq=}}, Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagare-zukuri|qq=}} (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City|qq=}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|qq=}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|qq=}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|qq=}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|qq=}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|qq=}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashii Shrine|qq=}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Bun’ei|qq=}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō Kiyomasa|qq=}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karatsu ware|qq=}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|qq=}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|qq=}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumoto Castle|qq=}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Disambiguation}} {{translated page|ja|聖母宮|version=89510503|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:Completed disambiguations]] Shrines]] [[ja:聖母宮]] [[en:Seibo Shrines]] [[ja:中津神社]] [[sn:Seibo Shrine]] [[sn:eibo Shrine]]

The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City|qq=}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|qq=}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|qq=}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|qq=}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|qq=}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|qq=}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashii Shrine|qq=}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Bun’ei|qq=}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō Kiyomasa|qq=}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karatsu ware|qq=}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|qq=}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|qq=}} * {{ill|Katsumoto Castle|ja|勝本城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumoto Castle|qq=}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Seibo Shrine] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Disambiguation}} {{translated page|ja|聖母宮|version=89510503|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:Completed disambiguations]] [[Category:Seibo Shrines]] [[Category:Seibo Shrine forks]] [[Category:Seibo Shrine]] [[Category:All disambiguation pages]] }} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Seibo Shrines]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrines]] [[ja:聖母宮]] [[en:Seibo Shrines]] [[ja:中津神社]] [[sn:Seibo Shrine]] [[sn:eibo Shrine]]

Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashii Shrine|qq=}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Bun’ei|qq=}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō Kiyomasa|qq=}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karatsu ware|qq=}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|qq=}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary Castle|qq=}} ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Seibo Shrine''' (聖母宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Iki City|ja|壱岐市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City|qq=}}, Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|qq=}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|qq=}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|qq=}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|qq=}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|qq=}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashii Shrine|qq=}} in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}}, suggest that Seibo Shrine is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the {{ill|Battle of Bun’ei|ja|文永の役|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Bun’ei|qq=}}. The main gate was donated by {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|ja|加藤清正|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katō Kiyomasa|qq=}} during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from {{ill|Karatsu ware|ja|唐津焼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karatsu ware|qq=}} bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|qq=}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary |title=Seibo Shrine Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Seibo Shrine] and
