{{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of [[:en:Iki Province|Iki Province]], Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 | location = {{ill|Iki|qid=Q861519}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Shoumogu''' (聖母宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki City]], Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|qq=|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|qq=|1=kinzai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626047}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]] in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, suggest that Shoumogu is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the [[:en:Battle of Bun'ei|Battle of Bun’ei]]. The main gate was donated by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]] during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from [[:en:Karatsu ware|Karatsu ware]] bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|qq=|1=Iki Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429582}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|qq=|1=Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429593}} * [[:en:Katsumoto Castle|Katsumoto Castle]] ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of [[:en:Iki Province|Iki Province]], Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 | location = {{ill|Iki|WD=Q861519}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Shoumogu''' (聖母宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki City]], Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|qq=|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|qq=|1=kinzai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626047}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]] in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, suggest that Shoumogu is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the [[:en:Battle of Bun'ei|Battle of Bun’ei]]. The main gate was donated by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]] during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from [[:en:Karatsu ware|Karatsu ware]] bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|qq=|1=Iki Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429582}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary wikidata|WD=Q11429593}} * [[:en:Katsumoto Castle|Katsumoto Castle]] ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of [[:en:Iki Province|Iki Province]], Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 | location = {{ill|Iki|WD=Q861519}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Shoumogu''' (聖母宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki City]], Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|qq=|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|qq=|1=kinzai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626047}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]] in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, suggest that Shoumogu is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the [[:en:Battle of Bun'ei|Battle of Bun’ei]]. The main gate was donated by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]] during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from [[:en:Karatsu ware|Karatsu ware]] bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|qq=|1=Iki Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429582}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|qq=|1=Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429593}} * [[:en:Katsumoto Castle|Katsumoto Castle]] ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Shoumogu Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Shoumogu] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Shoumogu] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{disambiguation}} {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of [[:en:Iki Province|Iki Province]], Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 | location = {{ill|Iki|WD=Q861519}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Shoumogu''' (聖母宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki City]], Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|1=kinzai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626047}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]] in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, suggest that Shoumogu is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the [[:en:Battle of Bun'ei|Battle of Bun’ei]]. The main gate was donated by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]] during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from [[:en:Karatsu ware|Karatsu ware]] bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|1=Iki Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429582}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|1=Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429593}} * [[:en:Katsumoto Castle|Katsumoto Castle]] ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Shoumogu Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Shoumogu] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Shoumogu] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{disambiguation}} The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Shoumogu''' (聖母宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki City]], Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|1=kinzai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626047}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]] in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, suggest that Shoumogu is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the [[:en:Battle of Bun'ei|Battle of Bun’ei]]. The main gate was donated by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]] during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from [[:en:Karatsu ware|Karatsu ware]] bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|1=Iki Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429582}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|1=Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429593}} * [[:en:Katsumoto Castle|Katsumoto Castle]] ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Shoumogu Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Shoumogu] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Shoumogu] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{disambiguation}} {{translated page|ja|聖母宮|version=89510503|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:All disambiguation pages]] }} [[ja:聖母宮]] [[ja:中津神社]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Shoumogu}} [[Category:Special:LonelyPages November 4 2025]] Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|1=kinzai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626047}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]] in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, suggest that Shoumogu is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the [[:en:Battle of Bun'ei|Battle of Bun’ei]]. The main gate was donated by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]] during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from [[:en:Karatsu ware|Karatsu ware]] bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|1=Iki Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429582}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|1=Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429593}} * [[:en:Katsumoto Castle|Katsumoto Castle]] ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Shoumogu Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Shoumogu] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Shoumogu] (Nagasaki Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|1=kinzai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626047}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]] in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, suggest that Shoumogu is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the [[:en:Battle of Bun'ei|Battle of Bun’ei]]. The main gate was donated by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]] during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from [[:en:Karatsu ware|Karatsu ware]] bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|1=Iki Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429582}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|1=Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429593}} * [[:en:Katsumoto Castle|Katsumoto Castle]] ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Shoumogu Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Shoumogu] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Shoumogu] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{disambiguation}} {{translated page|ja|聖母宮|version=89510503|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of [[:en:Iki Province|Iki Province]], Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 | location = {{ill|Iki|WD=Q861519}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Shoumogu''' (聖母宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki City]], Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, suggest that Shoumogu is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the [[:en:Battle of Bun'ei|Battle of Bun’ei]]. The main gate was donated by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]] during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from [[:en:Karatsu ware|Karatsu ware]] bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|1=Iki Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429582}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|1=Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429593}} * [[:en:Katsumoto Castle|Katsumoto Castle]] ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Shoumogu Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Shoumogu] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Shoumogu] (Nagasaki Prefecture Cultural Heritage) {{commonscat|Shoumogu}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{disambiguation}} {{translated page|ja|聖母宮|version=89510503|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:commons:Category:Shoumogu]][[ja:聖母宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11611081]] Wikidata name: Shoumogu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Shoumogu | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG | location = [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki City]], Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|33.855111|N|129.690889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of [[:en:Iki Province|Iki Province]], Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 | location = {{ill|Iki|WD=Q861519}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Shoumogu''' (聖母宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki City]], Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|1=kinzai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626047}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]] in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, suggest that Shoumogu is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the [[:en:Battle of Bun'ei|Battle of Bun’ei]]. The main gate was donated by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]] during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from [[:en:Karatsu ware|Karatsu ware]] bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|1=Iki Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429582}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|1=Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429593}} * [[:en:Katsumoto Castle|Katsumoto Castle]] ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Shoumogu Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Shoumogu] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Shoumogu] (Nagasaki [[sn:commons:Category:Shoumogu]][[ja:聖母宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11611081]] Wikidata name: Shoumogu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Shoumogu | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Shoumogu Haiden.JPG | location = [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki City]], Katsumoto Town, Katsumotoura 554-2, [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|33.855111|N|129.690889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}}
{{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}} | type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha), Second Shrine of [[:en:Iki Province|Iki Province]], Former Village Shrine | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall Style = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] (Three-Bay Style) | alternate_name = | Pilgrimage = Iki Province’s Seven Great Shrines | festival = October 14 | location = {{ill|Iki|WD=Q861519}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} [[File:Shoumogu Torii Gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Shoumogu West gate.JPG|thumb|right|250px|West Gate]] [[File:Shoumogu Honden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Main Hall
The sides are shielded from rain and wind; only the roof is exposed.]] [[File:Bateiseki (Iki).JPG|thumb|right|250px|Horseshoe Stone
Said to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse.]] '''Shoumogu''' (聖母宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Iki, Nagasaki|Iki City]], Nagasaki Prefecture, in Katsumoto Town. Listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, it is considered a potential site of the Shikinaisha “Nakatsu Shrine.” However, another shrine currently bearing that name exists separately ({{ill|Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|中津神社 (壱岐市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu Shrine (Iki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). == Enshrined Deities == The shrine honors the following deities: * {{ill|Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja|息長足姫尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinaga-Tarashihime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神功皇后|1=Empress Jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q232803}} (Empress Jingū) * {{ill|Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|足仲彦尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarashinakatsuhiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仲哀天皇|1=Chūai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q179971}} (Emperor Chūai) * {{ill|Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|上筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|仲筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja|底筒男尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sokotsutsunoo no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (collectively known as {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神}}) The shrine is associated with Empress Jingū’s {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}} (conquest of the Three Han States). Known as a deity of victory due to her triumph, she is also venerated as a deity of safe childbirth, as she gave birth to Emperor Ōjin after her victorious return. == History == In the 9th year of Emperor Chūai’s reign (possibly 200 CE), Empress Jingū constructed a temporary palace ({{ill|Angū|ja|行宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angū|1=kinzai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626047}}) in Iki while awaiting favorable winds for her campaign. The area was named “Kazamoto” (Wind Origin) after the northward winds blew. Upon her victorious return in December of the same year, the area was renamed “Katsumoto” (Victory Origin). According to legend, 101,500 enemy heads were buried here. The palace fell into disuse until a miraculous event involving glowing objects from the sea led to the offering of mirrors and the enshrinement of Empress Jingū. In 717 CE (Yōrō 1), the shrine was formally established, and structures were built to pray for national protection. Over time, it gained prominence as one of Iki’s Seven Great Shrines and the second shrine of Iki Province. It also maintained strong connections to [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]] in Fukuoka. Despite historical disputes regarding its status as a Shikinaisha, many researchers and reports, such as the {{ill|Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft調査報告|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, suggest that Shoumogu is the original Nakatsu Shrine, while the current Nakatsu Shrine represents a later branch. The shrine’s name “Seibo” (Holy Mother) and its location in Nagasaki Prefecture often lead to misconceptions about connections with Christianity, but it is entirely unrelated. == Festivals == The annual festival spans five days and is Iki’s largest event. It includes the Goshinkō-sen, a boat procession listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|選択無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}}, which predicts harvests and catches for the coming year. On October 14, the final day, events such as the Daikagura (Great Kagura Dance) of Iki and a costume parade are held. The Katsumoto Port Festival on October 15 is often considered part of the shrine’s festivities, extending the celebration to six days. == Grounds == The shrine grounds feature a horseshoe stone believed to bear the hoofprint of Empress Jingū’s horse during the Three Han campaign. It is also historically significant as the landing site of the Mongol invasions during the [[:en:Battle of Bun'ei|Battle of Bun’ei]]. The main gate was donated by [[:en:Katō Kiyomasa|Katō Kiyomasa]] during Toyotomi Hideyoshi’s Korean campaigns. The surrounding stone walls were constructed under Katō’s orders as his troops awaited favorable winds, reflecting the shrine’s deep ties to maritime events. == Cultural Properties == (Dates of designation in parentheses) === National Selected Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Iki’s Boat Race Festival (February 2, 1991) === Nagasaki Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate (Structures, February 25, 2004) * Tea Jar (Craftwork, February 4, 1972) : A black-glazed four-eared jar from [[:en:Karatsu ware|Karatsu ware]] bearing the year “1592,” the oldest inscribed artifact of Karatsu ware. == Transportation == * {{ill|Iki Kōtsū Bus|ja|壱岐交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki Kōtsū Bus|1=Iki Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429582}} Gōnoura-Katsumoto Line, Nakao Bus Stop == Nearby == * Katsumoto Port * {{ill|Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|ja|壱岐市立勝本小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|1=Iki City Katsumoto Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429593}} * [[:en:Katsumoto Castle|Katsumoto Castle]] ruins == Further Reading == * Shirai Eiji and Toki Masanori, ''Jinja Jiten'', Tokyo-do Shuppan, 1979, p. 172 == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www4.ocn.ne.jp/~syomogu/ |title=Shoumogu Official Website |date=20010417092136}} * [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/502 Main Hall, West Gate, and South Gate of Shoumogu] and [http://www.pref.nagasaki.jp/bunkadb/index.php/view/503 Tea Jar of Shoumogu] (Nagasaki
